1.synthesis: The Antioch church has prophets and teachers—Barnabas, Simeon called Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen, and Saul (Acts 13:1). While they worship and fast, the Holy Spirit says: 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them' (Acts 13:2). After fasting and prayer, they lay hands on them and send them off (Acts 13:3). They travel to Cyprus and through the island to Paphos, where they meet the proconsul Sergius Paulus and a Jewish false prophet named Bar-Jesus (Elymas the magician) (Acts 13:6–8). Elymas opposes them, seeking to turn away the proconsul from the faith. Saul—now called Paul—filled with the Holy Spirit, pronounces temporary blindness on him; the proconsul sees this and believes (Acts 13:9–12).
1.historical_context: The Antioch church's leadership (v.1) is notably diverse: Simeon called Niger ('Black') may be of African origin; Lucius of Cyrene (North Africa); Manaen was a childhood companion of Herod Antipas—a remarkable social range from royal court to diaspora African. The Spirit's word 'set apart for me' (aphorisate) uses the same verb Paul uses for his own calling in Romans 1:1 (aphōrismenos) and Galatians 1:15. Sergius Paulus as proconsul of Cyprus is attested in an inscription. The shift from 'Saul' to 'Paul' (v.13) in the narrative marks his emergence as the leading figure—Paul is his Roman name, appropriate for his Gentile mission.
1.literary_structure: Chapter 13 marks the narrative turning point of Acts: the mission moves from Jerusalem-centered to Antioch-centered, from Peter-led to Paul-led. The Antioch commissioning is the first Spirit-directed missionary sending (as opposed to the scattered mission of ch.8); it models the apostolic pattern the rest of Acts follows. The Elymas episode is the first dramatic confrontation of the missionary journey and serves as a test case: the same power that delivered Peter from prison now strikes a Jewish sorcerer with blindness—an ironic reversal of Paul's own Damascus road experience.
1.theological_application: The commissioning of Barnabas and Saul by the Spirit through the worshiping and fasting community establishes the pattern for all mission: it originates with the Spirit's call, is confirmed through communal discernment, and is sent by the community's prayer and laying on of hands. The church does not commission apostles on its own authority but recognizes and confirms what the Spirit has already indicated. Elymas's opposition reveals that the gospel always encounters resistance from those whose power and livelihood depend on alternative spiritual authorities—and the pattern of that resistance will persist throughout Paul's journeys.
1.christology: Paul's striking of Elymas with blindness (v.11) is the reverse of Paul's own Damascus road experience and an act of judgment in the pattern of the prophets (2 Kings 6:18). The proconsul's belief 'when he saw what had occurred' (v.12) is faith responding to a sign—the same pattern as the Samaria mission. Paul is 'filled with the Holy Spirit' (v.9) for this confrontation, underscoring that his authority is the risen Christ's, not his own. The missionary journey begins with a Gentile ruler converted—a foretaste of Paul's ultimate mission before kings (Acts 9:15).

13.pericope_label: Paul and Barnabas at Pisidian Antioch
13.synthesis: From Paphos, Paul and his companions sail to Perga in Pamphylia, where John Mark leaves them and returns to Jerusalem (Acts 13:13). They travel on to Pisidian Antioch and attend the synagogue on the Sabbath (Acts 13:14). After the reading of the Law and the Prophets, the synagogue rulers invite them to speak a word of encouragement (Acts 13:15).
13.historical_context: John Mark's departure at Perga (v.13) is the seed of the Barnabas-Paul split in chapter 15; Paul views it as desertion. Pisidian Antioch (distinct from Syrian Antioch) was a Roman colony on the high plateau of Asia Minor, a significant administrative center with a substantial Jewish community. The synagogue service invited visiting teachers to speak 'a word of encouragement' (logos paraklēseōs)—the standard format for Sabbath teaching by qualified visitors. This invitation opens the way for Paul's longest recorded missionary sermon in Acts.
13.literary_structure: This brief transitional section sets up the pivotal sermon of Acts 13:16–41. The travel details (Paphos → Perga → Pisidian Antioch) trace the northward movement of the mission from the coast to the interior highlands. John Mark's departure is noted without comment but will become significant later; Luke records it to explain the tension in ch.15 and to establish that from this point, Paul is the decisive leader. The synagogue invitation is the opening the mission needed: access to the Jewish community and Gentile God-fearers simultaneously.
13.theological_application: The synagogue setting—beginning each new city's mission in the Jewish community—reflects Paul's own stated principle: 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek' (Romans 1:16). This is not merely pragmatic (synagogues provide ready audiences) but theological: salvation comes 'from the Jews' (John 4:22), and the gospel's first claim is on the covenant people. The 'word of encouragement' invited is exactly what Paul brings—not condemnation but the fulfillment of Israel's ancient hope in the crucified and risen Jesus. The note that John Mark departed at Perga and returned to Jerusalem (v.13) invites reflection on the cost of missionary persistence: John Mark's departure does not disqualify him permanently (Paul will later commend him in 2 Timothy 4:11), but it registers as a failure of this particular juncture. The church must distinguish between those who have abandoned the mission entirely and those who have failed at a given moment and can be restored—realism about present failure and hope for future restoration are both pastoral necessities, and neither cancels the other.
13.christology: The journey to Pisidian Antioch follows the Spirit's leading through the human decisions of travel and the providential opportunity of the synagogue invitation. The risen Christ who commissioned Paul ('to carry my name before the Gentiles,' Acts 9:15) opens doors through the ordinary structures of diaspora Jewish worship—the synagogue becomes the vehicle for his name to be proclaimed in the interior of Asia Minor, beginning the geographical expansion that will eventually reach Rome.

16.synthesis: Paul stands and motions for silence, addressing both the Jewish audience and the Gentile God-fearers. He rehearses Israel's history—the exodus, the wilderness, the conquest, the judges, Saul, and David—leading to the promise of a Savior from David's line (Acts 13:16–25). Though the rulers of Jerusalem did not recognize Jesus or understand the prophets, they fulfilled them by condemning him; Pilate found no guilt but killed him; God raised him on the third day, and he appeared to witnesses (Acts 13:26–31). The sermon moves to scriptural proof: Psalm 2:7 ('You are my Son, today I have begotten you'), Isaiah 55:3 (the holy and sure blessings of David), and Psalm 16:10 ('You will not let your Holy One see corruption')—all fulfilled in the resurrection of the one David could not be, since David died and saw corruption (Acts 13:32–37). Let it be known: through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed, and everyone who believes is justified from everything from which the law of Moses could not justify (Acts 13:38–39).
16.historical_context: The Pisidian Antioch sermon is Paul's most extensive sermon in Acts and serves as the template for his synagogue proclamation. Its historical review parallels Stephen's speech but culminates differently: not in indictment but in gospel offer. The scriptural proofs (Psalm 2, Isaiah 55, Psalm 16) are the same texts Peter used at Pentecost (Psalm 16) and the early church's standard resurrection apologetics. The justification language of vv.38–39—'justified from everything from which the law of Moses could not justify'—is the first occurrence of Paul's distinctive justification theology in Acts and anticipates the letters to Galatia and Rome.
16.literary_structure: The sermon follows the pattern of Acts 2 and Acts 7: historical review → event (death and resurrection) → scriptural proof → call to response. Paul's historical review (vv.16–25) is more compressed than Stephen's and serves differently—not to indict but to prepare the audience to recognize Jesus as the promised Davidic deliverer. The scriptural proof section (vv.32–37) is tightly argued: each psalm points to one who cannot be David (who died), therefore points to Jesus (who rose without corruption). The sermon's close (vv.38–41) is an offer and a warning—a warning citing Habakkuk 1:5 about those who do not believe what God is doing in their generation.
16.theological_application: The Pisidian Antioch sermon is the model of gospel proclamation that connects to the audience's existing framework (Israel's history, the scriptures, the expectation of David's promise) before presenting its fulfillment in Jesus' death and resurrection. The justification claim—that what the law could not accomplish, faith in the risen Jesus accomplishes—is the gospel's most direct challenge to every system of earning standing before God. The church's proclamation must include this: forgiveness is not achievable by religious performance but is given freely to all who believe, through the one who bore the law's condemnation and rose to justify the ungodly.
16.christology: 'Through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you, and by him everyone who believes is justified from everything from which the law of Moses could not justify you' (Acts 13:38–39)—Paul's christological claim at Pisidian Antioch is the most explicit statement of the cross's salvific effect in the Acts sermons. The risen Jesus is the one through whom forgiveness flows, the one whose resurrection is the proof of the scriptures' fulfillment, the Davidic heir who received what David could not—exemption from corruption. This is the gospel Paul will preach to the ends of the earth.

42.synthesis: As Paul and Barnabas leave, the people beg for the word on the next Sabbath. Almost the whole city gathers; the Jews see the crowds and are filled with jealousy, contradicting Paul and reviling him (Acts 13:44–45). Paul and Barnabas respond boldly: 'It was necessary that the word of God be spoken first to you. Since you thrust it aside and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we are turning to the Gentiles' (Acts 13:46). They cite Isaiah 49:6: 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth' (Acts 13:47). The Gentiles rejoice and glorify the word of the Lord; as many as were appointed to eternal life believe (Acts 13:48). The word of the Lord spreads throughout the region. The Jews incite persecution; Paul and Barnabas are expelled but shake the dust from their feet. The disciples are filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit (Acts 13:49–52).
42.historical_context: The pattern of synagogue proclamation → initial interest → Jewish rejection → turn to Gentiles will repeat in Corinth (18:6) and Rome (28:28)—it is the structural arc of Paul's entire mission in Acts. The Isaiah 49:6 citation is significant: the Servant's mission to be a light to the Gentiles was addressed to Israel by God; Paul applies it to his own apostolic mission, identifying himself (and Barnabas) with the Servant's vocation. 'As many as were appointed to eternal life believed' (v.48) is Luke's only explicitly predestinarian statement, placing ultimate causation in divine appointment rather than human decision—while also presenting faith as the genuine response of those appointed.
42.literary_structure: The rejection/turn-to-Gentiles pattern is the first explicit statement of a principle that Paul will repeat. The jealousy of the Jews (v.45) contrasts with the joy of the Gentiles (v.48)—Luke's ironic reversal of expected responses. The disciples' joy and filling with the Holy Spirit at the chapter's close (v.52) despite expulsion mirrors the pattern of Acts 8: persecution accelerates expansion rather than stopping it. The citation of Isaiah 49:6 is the theological anchor: the Gentile mission is not Paul's innovation but the fulfillment of Israel's vocation as light to the nations.
42.theological_application: 'Appointed to eternal life' (v.48) is among the most discussed phrases in Acts, raising questions about divine sovereignty and human responsibility. Luke's presentation holds both: the word goes out universally, people choose to believe or reject, and the ultimate ground of faith is divine appointment. This twin emphasis—genuine human response and divine initiative—is characteristic of biblical soteriology rather than a theological problem to be resolved by choosing one side. The church's mission proceeds with confidence precisely because 'as many as were appointed' will believe—the mission is not at risk of total failure, even when it encounters rejection.
42.christology: The Isaiah 49:6 citation—'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth' (Acts 13:47)—applies to the apostolic mission a vocation that Isaiah addressed to the Servant/Israel. The risen Christ, the true Servant, has commissioned Paul to extend his own Servant-ministry to the Gentiles. Paul's mission is the risen Christ's continuing mission: the light that Jesus called himself ('I am the light of the world,' John 8:12) is extended to the ends of the earth through those he sends. The word of the Lord spreading (v.49) is the risen Christ's reign expanding.
